[Page 1]

Consistent and Transferable Force Fields for Statistical Copolymer Systems at the Mesoscale R. L. Nkepsu Mbitou, F. Goujon,* A. Dequidt,* B. Latour, J. Devémy, R. Blaak, N. Martzel, C. Emeriau-Viard, J. Tchoufag, S. Garruchet, E. Munch, P. Hauret, and P. Malfreyt

Downloaded via KYOTO UNIV on October 10, 2022 at 00:39:05 (UTC). See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

Cite This: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.jctc.2c00945

sı Supporting Information *

ABSTRACT: The statistical trajectory matching (STM) method was applied successfully to derive coarse grain (CG) models for bulk properties of homopolymers. The extension of the methodology for building CG models for statistical copolymer systems is much more challenging. We present here the strategy for developing CG models for styrene−butadiene−rubber, and we compare the quality of the resulting CG force fields on the structure and thermodynamics at different chemical compositions. The CG models are used through the use of a genuine mesoscopic method called the dissipative particle dynamics method and compared to high-resolution molecular dynamics simulations. We conclude that the STM method is able to produce coarse-grained potentials that are transferable in composition by using only a few reference systems. Additionally, this methodology can be applied on any copolymer system.

1. INTRODUCTION The field of computer simulation in polymer science has been at the origin of many developments of coarse grain (CG) models and corresponding mesoscopic simulation methods.1−61 Indeed, polymer chains are natural candidates for coarse graining: slow relaxation and chains formed by covalent bonding of a large number of identical monomers. A number of generic CG models7−16 with low resolutions have been applied in the past to reproduce universal (scaling laws) properties of polymer melts, but the quantitative prediction of properties as a function of the polymer chemistry represents a still growing and challenging activity of research. The inclusion of molecular details leads to the so-called realistic CG models can be achieved through top-down and bottom-up parameterization schemes. Top-down approaches derive parameters from macroscopic properties. By imposing simple interaction potentials described by few parameters, these interactions can be tuned to reproduce various properties such as compressibility, diffusion, surface tension, density, and radius of gyration.17−19 Complex systems involving many different CG particles can be parameterized by using equation of states20−23 or by reproducing the free energy of each a type of interaction.24 Bottom-up approaches use the configurations at the atomistic level obtained from molecular dynamics (MD) or Monte Carlo simulation methods to develop interaction forces and parameters of the realistic CG models.25−30 The bottom-up approaches transfer information from a system with an atomistic resolution to a system described with a lower resolution. This is © XXXX American Chemical Society

the multi-scale aspect of the approach. Among the bottom-up approach, the iterative Boltzmann inversion (IBI) method31 is based on the polymer structure and consists in determining an effective pair potential for an ensemble of identical particles from the corresponding radial distribution function (RDF) using the potential of mean force approach.32 The IBI method has some limitations concerning its transferability to heterogeneous systems as the RDF requires an isotropic environment. An alternative to the IBI method is the force matching (FM) method,33−38 which is based upon the analysis of the instantaneous forces on the beads. Many bottom-up coarse-graining methodologies are interested on the notion of transferability, for the developed CG force field (FF).39−48 It is an open and long-debated topic, and its main purpose is to get a unique generic mesoscopic potential that attempts to reproduce static and dynamic properties from different systems of interest. Such systems are classified by state points, which can refer, for instance, to thermodynamic (temperature, pressure, concentration, and pH49), chemical (CG bead types regarding the chemical nature of repeat units), and local environment (e.g., from bulk to interfacial regions of Received: September 18, 2022

[Page 2]

CG systems) conditions. In the context of thermodynamic transferability, a CG potential should accurately model multiple systems in various thermodynamic states. To just enumerate a few work examples among the considerable efforts made to this day,50−55 Krishna et al.56 extended the FM methodology for transferring CG potentials across different temperatures. Xia et al.57 constructed a temperature transferable CG model for atactic polystyrene (PS) by using both the IBI approach for bonded potentials and the hard LJ 12-6 potential for the nonbonded interaction potential. Lastly, the work of Ruza et al.58 used machine learning to train one neural network model on atomistic MD simulations to obtain a temperature transferable many-body potential of mean forces for ionic liquids. Concerning the chemical (or composition) transferability, some studies have been performed on the mixture of small molecules using bottom-up CG frameworks such as IBI,59,60 extended FM,61−64 pressure matching,65−67 relative entropy,68,69 conditional reversible work (CRW),70,71 and integral equation coarse graining (IECG).72 Recently, we have developed a new bottom-up statistical trajectory matching (STM) method73,74 relying on a Bayesian approach to obtain the parameters of the CG models. The fact that it gives the most probable potential that reproduces a given trajectory is an advantage over the IBI method as the optimization process of the IBI method can lead to a nonunicity of the resulting potential. The fact that the trajectory is used as a reference allows us to develop rigorous pressuretransferable potentials without considering additional interactions. This was used to develop a multi-temperature, multipressure CG FF75 for dissipative particle dynamics (DPD)76,77 simulations of cis-1,4-polybutadiene, allowing us to predict properties of various polymer melts.75,78−80 We then discussed the effect of increasing the degree of coarse-graining on the reproduction of the melt density and radius of gyration of the chains:78 the greater the degree of coarse graining, the greater the compressibility of the CG models, and the lower the equilibrium density. For a degree of coarse graining equal to 5 (1 bead = 5 monomers), it was impossible to reproduce the experimental density and radius of gyration. With this resolution, the grain shape fluctuates in proportions that cannot be ignored and with a characteristic time much greater than the simulation time step,78 and its shape is never spherical. Contrary to the top-down approaches, the bottom-up coarse graining of complex system involving different particle types is a recent topic.48,69,81 The coarse graining of copolymer systems that focus on the transferability in composition has not yet been investigated except for a recent work60 that uses the IBI method. In this paper, we propose a new strategy to obtain mesoscopic FFs that are consistent and transferable to any random copolymer system. The easy inclusion of pressure effects in the STM method allows us to develop robust pairwise potentials without considering ad hoc corrections or density-dependent nonbonded interactions. We apply the STM method to styrene−butadiene rubber (SBR) with respect to chemical composition as this copolymer material is of industrial interest, especially in the tire industry. Therefore, we organize the paper as follows: in Section 1, we very briefly recall the STM method and the theoretical idea to combine a set of mesoscopic FFs provided each by the STM process. In Section 2, we list the simulation details necessary to produce the MD trajectories of the targeted SBR systems. In Section 3, we present and discuss the obtained CG potentials for several combined CG FFs and

the static properties between the high-resolution MD reference trajectories and their corresponding CG DPD models.

2. METHODOLOGY 2.1. Statistical Trajectory Matching. The FF parameters of the CG model were obtained using the STM method.73 Here, we briefly recall the workings of this method. The idea is to find the optimal CG FF parameters by observing and trying to reproduce the dynamics of an atomistic system. The first step is therefore to obtain a trajectory of the CGs to serve as the ground truth. This CG trajectory is obtained from a high-resolution trajectory generated by MD simulation, by grouping atoms into grains located at the center of mass of the group of atoms. Any CG FF used to perform CG simulations of this same system has a (minuscule) probability to generate exactly this reference trajectory. By assuming the functional form of the CG FF, it may be possible to express analytically this probability and to find the parameters that maximize it. The STM method thus looks for the most likely CG model in the sense that the optimized model is the one having the highest probability to generate exactly the sequence of configurations of the reference CG trajectory. The second step is to compute the total force on each grain at each step of the trajectory. Let us denote by Ft the 3N vector of forces on the N grains. Rt is the 3N vector of the grain positions (center of mass of the group of atoms) and Vt is the 3N vector of the grain velocities. If we want to ensure that our CG simulation follows exactly the reference trajectory, we need to have Vt =

Note that as the box size fluctuates, Rt−Δt has to be rescaled to the box at time t. Therefore, no pressure correction is needed. The simulation at the CG level follows the equations of DPD, which can be formally written as Ft = FtC + FtD + FtR

Of course, extending this computation to more than two trajectories is straightforward. The friction magnitude can also be computed for the combined model as in ref 74. 2.3. Simulation Protocol. 2.3.1. Simulation Boxes. A total of 18 different bulks of homopolymer and copolymer systems are polymerized (randomly, for copolymers) through the use of four different repeating units: 1,4-cis-butadiene (C), 1,4-transbutadiene (T), 1,2-vinyl-butadiene (V), and styrene (S). Likewise, the magnitude of the friction can also be optimized during the STM calculations.74 2.2. Theory on Combining FFs at the Mesoscale. The STM method can combine several independent trajectories. This means that it can provide the single set of parameters that lead to the highest probability of generating several reference trajectories. This has already been used to obtain a CG FF transferable in pressure and temperature.74,75 In this work, we show how to combine trajectories of different systems, aiming at obtaining an FF transferable in composition. The idea is again to compute and maximize the probability that a single model with parameters x would generate exactly all the target reference trajectories. For example, let us suppose that we have a reference trajectory 1 of grains of kinds A and B and another trajectory 2 of grains of kinds B and C. We can get the optimal parameters for the AA, AB, and BB interactions from trajectory 1. Likewise, we obtain the parameters for the BB, BC, and CC interactions from trajectory 2 . Thus, we have two sets of parameters for the BB interaction. For obtaining a single set of parameters working at best in both circumstances, we have to consider for each trajectory the whole set of parameters {xAA, xAB, xBB, xBC, xCC}. Technically, when the sum of eq 9 is already computed for each trajectory, the corresponding sum for the combined model is obtained by adding them and taking care to add together the columns/rows associated with the same unknown parameter. This can be achieved using non-square “permutation” matrices. If P1 denotes the ptot × p1 matrix mapping the indices from the subset of parameters used in 1 to the full set of parameters, and P2 denotes the equivalent mapping for 2 * * + 1 + P2Q ) x = (PS 1 1P1 + P2S2P2 ) (PQ

describes the systems used in this work. In the following, we use the notation AB for a two monomer composition, ABC for three monomers, and ABCD for four monomers. The copolymers are composed of all possible combinations from two to four different monomers, with the same molar fraction for each monomer (50% for AB, 33% for ABC, and 25% for ABCD copolymers). In the case of ABCD copolymers, we added three compositions that cover the range of the styrene proportion used in the rubber industry. They are some of the main systems of industrial interest that we aim to reproduce. Each cubic simulation box consists of a periodic amorphous cell of 40 all-

with Si =

{1,2}

[Page 4]

atom (AA) polymer chains, where each chain holds 20 repeating units. Each chain in the initial configuration is generated using the Material Studio package by imposing the bond and angle values and allowing dihedral to take random values. The chain is then accepted if no atoms are too close to each other. Each system is finally validated if the average end-to-end distance is close to the imposed value. The atomistic trajectories are then generated by MD simulations using the LAMMPS software.82 The same systems will then be simulated using CG DPD simulations. 2.3.2. Atomistic Trajectories. The MD simulations were carried using the generalized Amber force field83 (GAFF) to model at the AA scale all the target bulks of polymer systems. The parameters of this atomistic FF are given in Table S1 of the Supporting Information. We performed 10, 10, and 100 ns simulations, respectively, for equilibration, production (to record atomistic trajectories every 25 time steps for the STM procedure), and long-production (to record atomistic reference trajectories every 2500 time steps) stages by using a time step of 2 fs. The short equilibration period was determined by checking the convergence of the density. The radius of gyration and the end-to-end distance distributions were split into 10 superblocks during the long-production simulations to ensure that the chains were correctly equilibrated. The integration of the equations of motion was performed using the standard velocity Verlet algorithm.84 We used the SHAKE algorithm85 to constrain all the C−H covalent bonds of polymer chains. The Lennard-Jones crossing terms, which involve different atoms i and j, were evaluated using the Lorentz−Berthelot mixing rules (i.e., ij = i j and σij = (σi + σj)/2, where σ and ε are the energy parameter and the diameter of i and j atom types, respectively). The MD simulations were performed in the constant NPT ensemble (where N, P, and T represent the number of atoms, the pressure, and the temperature, respectively), and we chose the Nosé−Hoover86,87 thermostat and barostat algorithm to maintain the temperature at 300 K and the pressure at 0.1 MPa. The thermostat and the barostat relaxation times of that algorithm were set at 200 and 2000 fs, respectively. The cutoff radius was fixed to 12 Å for Lennard-Jones and Coulombic (at long-range) interactions, and the (1−2), (1−3), and (1−4) weighting factors for such interactions were set to 0.0, 0.0, and 0.5, respectively. The periodic boundary conditions were applied in the three directions. The Coulombic interactions at a long range were handled using the particle−particle−particle− mesh 3D method.88 2.3.3. Mapping Procedure of the Trajectories. Trajectories of each type of polymer systems listed in Table 1 were recorded every Δt = 50 fs during the MD production phase as it is the chosen DPD time step. This value has shown to be a good compromise between the wanted performance of the CG simulations and the need to avoid unwanted crossing between the chains that would break the topology of the system.78 During the following MD long production, configurations are stored every 5 ps to calculate the reference static properties. We mapped each repeating unit into one CG for the different MD trajectories in order to get the MD coarse-grained (MDCG) ones (see Figure 2). We then defined the x, y, and z coordinates of the CG beads as the center of mass of all the constitutive atoms of each type of repeating units. Therefore, this process effectively reduces the number of particles in any system of study by a factor 10 and 16, respectively, for all structural isomers of butadiene and styrene.

Figure 2. (a) Summary of the monomer formulas and their identification code used in this work. The types used in the MD simulations and listed in the Supporting Information are shown in red on each different atom. (b,c) Illustration of the coarse graining procedure applied on the SBR25 system (25% of each monomer, see Table 1): (b) all-atom configuration and (c) CG mapping on monomers leading to four distinct CG particles (red: C, blue: T, yellow: V, and green: S).

The grain position matches the center of mass of the corresponding group of atoms over the trajectories. Moreover, the velocity of any grain is obtained from the difference between

interaction and θij is an ij-dependent Gaussian random number with zero mean and unit variance. The total nonbonded force between two particles is the sum of the conservative, dissipative, and random forces. The forces are integrated using a standard velocity Verlet algorithm. The timestep Δt = 50 fs corresponds to the interval between the configurations used by the STM method. 2.3.5. Parameterization of CG Potentials. Using the reference trajectory recorded every time step Δt, this method finds the optimal CG FF parameters so that as they maximize some likelihood function,73 the resulting model will reproduce the reference trajectory. CG potentials of 18 polymer systems (according to Table 1) were optimized independently using their corresponding MDCG reference trajectory. Then, to guarantee the transferability in chemical composition, we developed a list (Table 2)

two consecutive positions separated by a time step. Furthermore, the Newton’s equation of motion is used to compute the total force acting on any CG at each time step and derived from its acceleration. 2.3.4. CG Models. We chose the STM approach73−75,78 to derive the CG DPD potentials, which are composed of conservative (intramolecular bonding and bending potentials and nonbonded pairwise forces) and dissipative potential parameters.89,90 For conservative potentials, we first modeled the bonds and angles by the following harmonic potentials Eijb = kijb(r

rij0)2 , Eijk = kijk (

0 2 ijk)

where r0ij and θ0ijk are, respectively, the equilibrium distance of any pair of CG beads ij and the equilibrium angle of any three represent their consecutive CG beads ijk. kijb and kijk corresponding bond and angle elastic constants, respectively. This means that two parameters are required to represent any type of bonds and angles in any polymer systems. Therefore, they are included in such systems to account for polymer chain stiffness and flexibility. Second, the nonbonded forces were parameterized by tabulated pair potentials. The pairwise nonbonded force is interpolated with cubic splines using a sampling of 10 distance points (LAMMPS pair style “table”). The distances are listed in the Supporting Information. We note that the choice of having 10 distance points results from a trade-off between having a high precision versus having few fit parameters and smooth curves. The conservative cutoff distance was set to rc = 20 Å, as discussed in the previous section, and the weighting coefficients for pairwise conservative energies and forces between 1−2 (bond) and 1−3 (angle) patterns were set to 0.0 and 0.5, respectively. The non-conservative interaction between any pair of beads ij was governed by the dissipative potential, and it uses the following classical DPD additive force f Dj i ij jj1 jj fj i = m o 0 for r

of multi-chemical composition models (multi-CC) by combining the optimal CG potential parameters. Finally, we propose six multi-CC (from M1 to M6) models that combine 5, 4, 11, 15, 14, and 18 CG models, respectively. The six test cases have been chosen by considering the following criteria: • M1: targeted copolymer systems, that is, at least three different monomers (five reference simulations needed) • M2: the minimum number of reference systems (four reference simulations needed) • M3: M1 with additional information from AB copolymers (11 reference simulations needed) • M4: M3 with additional information from homopolymers (15 reference simulations needed) • M5: M3 with additional information from four ABCD compositions (14 reference simulations needed) • M6: M4 with additional information from four ABCD compositions (18 reference simulations needed)

where γ is the friction coefficient, vij = vi − vj is the relative velocity, and eij represents the unit vector in the direction from CG bead i to CG bead j. We chose to use the same friction coefficient γ for any nonbonded pairwise bead interaction, where its optimal value was obtained during the STM optimization of CG reference trajectories. This is an approximation as one would expect different monomers to have specific friction coefficients. We chose to use a single value for γ because the dynamical properties still fail to be reproduced accurately by the STM method. The dissipative interaction takes part until the relative distance r between any pair of beads ij becomes greater than the dissipative cutoff rd (set to 10 Å). To form a DPD thermostat, we added to such a dissipative force the additive random one f Rj i ij r yzz eij for r < rd zz ij o jjjj1 rd { fj i = m n 0 for r rd

3. RESULTS AND DISCUSSION 3.1. Coarse-Grained Potentials. We first examine the bond and angle interactions described by eq 13 generated by the STM method for the six test cases of combined potentials. The complete list of the kb, r0, kθ, and θ0 parameters can be found in Table S2 of the Supporting Information. The average value of the spring constant kb ranges from 1.89 kcal mol−1 Å−2 for the V−V interaction to 0.47 for the S−S interaction. An opposite progression is observed for the average equilibrium distance r0, which ranges from 3.58 Å for the V−V interaction up to 6.19 for the S−S interaction. This progression is expected as the vinyl monomer has the smallest number of carbons in the main chain, leading to a narrow distribution of V−

where = 2kBT , which is imposed by the fluctuation− dissipation theorem,91 is the amplitude of this random

[Page 6]

V distances. The styrene−styrene bonded interaction has the smallest spring constant because of the larger size of the monomer, allowing larger fluctuations around the largest r0 value. The values of the bond parameters are very similar between the six test cases, with a standard deviation of less than 3% for both kb and r0. This shows a good consistency of the STM method in generating the combined potential. Indeed, one may expect that the bond interactions must be almost the same for every model as they are only dependent on the internal chain structure only. The bending parameters kθ and θ0 show some similarity between the M1−M6 test cases, with a standard deviation of less than 10% for most of the angle types. Other angles such as S− V−S or V−S−V have very different values for kθ in the M1 and M2 cases on one side and M3−6 cases on the other side. However, this is coherent with the fact that the CG bending potential is only a small correction to the 1−3 nonbonded interactions. The nonbonded interactions corresponding to the 10 different pairs of CG particles for the M1 potential are shown in Figure 3a. The larger size of the styrene S monomer creates three groups: C, T, and V monomers and their cross interactions, the S monomer, and cross interactions with S. These three main behaviors occur both on the well depth and on the excluded volume caused by the repulsive part. The S−S attraction at the minimum is more important due to the large number of van der Waals interactions occurring between the benzene rings. The STM method applied on various types of CG particles at once allows a good reproduction of the physics and selectivity of the monomers. We note that all the pair interactions exhibit a minimum at approximately the same distance r ≈ 12 A, which is larger than the average carbon−carbon distance due to the choice of the monomer center of mass as the position of the CG particle. The cutoff radius can be imposed at any value as an input of the STM method. A small cutoff is always preferred, as it reduces the computational cost of the simulations. However, nonrealistic results are obtained both for nonbonded interactions and calculated properties if a cutoff lower than 16 Å is considered. This value is dependent of the CG level as all atom interactions between CG particles have to contribute to the CG interactions. For a CG level of 1 (one monomer per CG particle), we chose rcut = 20 Å as a value large enough to include the necessary interactions and small enough to avoid a large cost in computation.78 Figure 3b,c shows the comparison of the six test cases for the C−V and C−S nonbonded interactions, respectively. At first sight, the different CG potentials are very similar for one given pair, which is a good test of consistency of the STM method and the choice of the conservative cutoff radius. Interestingly, the test cases M5 and M6 exhibit a slightly deeper well and a harder repulsion for short distances. This behavior is observed for every pair type and seems to be correlated to the inclusion of the four ABCD compositions in the combined potentials (used only for M5 and M6). 3.2. Structure. We now focus on the RDF to compare the structure of the CG systems with the reference MD simulations. Figure 4a shows the RDF of the C−C pair in the SBR25 system for the six CG combined potentials. The RDF do not include the 1−2 bonded pairs. The reference RDF is obtained from the coarse-grained MD trajectory. The CG profiles are very similar, with a good reproduction of the first peak and the first minimum

Figure 3. (a) Nonbonded interaction potential energies obtained for the CG potential type M1; (b) nonbonded potential of the C−V interaction obtained for the six test cases; and (c) nonbonded potential of the C−S interaction obtained for the six test cases.

and a slight underestimation of the first peak intensity. The M6 case shows a higher peak closer to the MD reference. Figure 4b shows the RDF for the V−V structure, which is the interaction that exhibits the worst comparison with MD. The first peak is well-reproduced by each potential with a slight overestimation of the intensity of the first peak, but the first minimum is overestimated. This could be due to the fact that the vinyl group cannot be easily reproduced by a spherical shape: the second layer is then located at a larger distance. The RDF of the other pairs are well-reproduced and show no more difference with MD than the C−C pair shown in Figure 4a. This is a satisfying result as the STM method does not use the RDF as an

[Page 7]

Figure 4. RDFs calculated between CG particles: (a) comparison of the six combined potentials with MD for the Cis−Cis pair in the SBR25 system; (b) comparison of the six combined potentials with MD for the Vin−Vin pair in the SBR25 system; (c) details of the Cis−Cis pair in four systems using the M1 combined potential; and (d) details of each pair using the M1 combined potential in the SBR25 system.

input to generate CG potentials. Additionally, all test cases give close results, showing a good consistency of the combination of CG potentials. Figure 4c is a detailed view of the C−C RDF obtained for four different systems with the M1 combined potential. The general aspect of the C−C structure is kept in the four different environments. We observe a stronger structuration in the case of the copolymer containing a larger proportion of the Cis monomer (50% for CT and 33% for CTV), but this effect is not reproduced for cis-PB. As can be seen in the next section, the homopolymer are poorly reproduced by combined copolymer potentials, so we can exclude the cis-PB from our analysis. Finally, we plot the 10 different RDFs obtained in the SBR25 system with the M6 potential in Figure 4d. The respective sizes of the monomers seen in the nonbonded potentials are wellreproduced, with a shift of 2 Å in the first peak between Vin−Vin and Sty−Sty. The STM method is able to conserve the specific local structure around each type of monomer, which might allow a good transferability in composition for the combined potentials. Additionally, we found that the single fits (i.e., applying the STM method separately on each single system, not shown) do not give better results. The general structure of the polymer chains is then studied by plotting the distribution of the chain radius of gyration RG in Figure 5a and the end-to-end distance Ree in Figure 5b for the SBR25 system. The long-production MD simulation is used as a reference and exhibits multiple peaks due to the relatively short simulation time compared to the characteristic time of the chain dynamics. Note that we used as the MD reference the short input simulations of the STM method, thus expecting this result.

Figure 5. Comparison of the six combined potentials with MD for the SBR25 system for (a) radius of gyration and (b) end-to-end distance of the CG polymer chains.

This is not a problem, however, as the STM method is designed to capture a small part of the system trajectory. Despite the fact that the average end-to-end distance is imposed by the initial configuration, the relatively small number of chains (40) results in sharp peaks on the Ree and RG distributions as each chain is only slightly relaxed during the 100 ns run. The CG simulations give smooth distributions that are similar for each test case. It is tricky to compare the CG model with a single MD simulation that exhibits such peaks in the distributions. As a consequence, we performed five distinct MD runs using different random seeds for the creation of the initial configurations. The RG and Ree distributions are then averaged over these five runs to give a better reference for CG comparison (see Figure S1 in the Supporting Information). Considering the average MD reference, there is a good qualitative reproduction of the MD simulations in terms of the width and the position of the maximum of the distributions. The mean-square displacement and the end-to-end vector decorrelation are shown in Figure S2 of the Supporting Information. The rapid CG dynamics is not dependent on the test case and allows the chains to completely decorrelate, as

opposed to MD. The existing CG methods exhibit a well-known increase in the chain dynamics, which allows us here to sample the chain structure more rapidly. The six test cases combined potential gives very similar results. The average values of RG, Ree, and R2ee/R2G obtained with the M1 potential for all the copolymer systems are summarized in Table 3. The selectivity of the polymer composition is quite poor, with only a qualitative agreement between MD and DPD for both RG and Ree. The relation R2ee/R2G is smoothed in the CG simulations and does not discriminate well between the various chain compositions. This can be attributed to the small size and simulation time of the reference MD simulations. 3.3. Densities. To complete our analysis of the combined CG FFs, we focus on the average density of all the systems. As the STM method does not use explicitly the pressure as an input, the ability to accurately reproduce the density of polymer bulks is a strong test for the method. Figure 6 shows the relative error in density E (see eq 16) obtained between CG and MD simulations.

[Page 9]

E=

CG

The obtained CG potentials showed a qualitative similarity between the test cases, with a good reproduction of the expected physics of the various CG particles such as size, intensity of the nonbonded and bonded interactions, and the local structure. The different test cases are consistent with each other and are able to discriminate between the various monomer compositions and environments. The density of the polymer bulks at the mesoscale were then confronted with the corresponding reference high-definition MD trajectories as it is very sensitive to the CG potential used. The consistency of the STM method was examined on this property as it is not used as an input for the coarse graining procedure. The ABC and ABCD copolymer systems are very accurately reproduced using all the test cases, leading us to select only a few reference chemical compositions as a reference. These combined potentials are almost as efficient as individual STM fits for each single system. We conclude that the STM method is able to combine several reference systems into a single, transferable FF that can accurately reproduce the static properties of bulk copolymer systems. Some extension of the method could be interesting for other complex systems such as biomolecules. This raises several issues as the STM method aims at reproducing the trajectory of the CG particles: top-down approaches based on free energy seem to be better candidates for the coarse graining of such systems. However, an interesting improvement of the STM method could be the use of non -pherical particles, allowing a better reproduction of the various monomer shapes.

× 100

The systems listed in Table 1 are shown on the x-axis in an increasing order of the number of different monomers. Each test case from M1 to M6 is shown for each system. The single fit is the result of the STM method applied individually on each system. It is used as a reference for the maximum efficiency of the STM method that could be obtained with no transferability in composition. Density values are listed with their fluctuations in Table S3 of the Supporting Information. The density fluctuations are smaller than the symbols and are thus not displayed in Figure 6. As expected, the single fit CG potentials show the best reproduction of the density, with a relative error E of less than 5% except for the PS and TS polymers. However, each of the 18 systems is needed an individual fit to obtain these results, which is not the case for the combined potentials. We note that the CG density is almost always underestimated, as has been discussed in previous studies.75,78 The homopolymers are poorly reproduced by the combined potentials. This may be due to the amount of useless information that is treated by the STM method in this particular case, therefore decreasing the statistics of the only interaction considered in homopolymers. As a consequence, the combination of the copolymer CG FFs is not advised for the simulation of homopolymers. This is not a problem as our goal is to include at least two monomer types in the CG simulations. The ABC and ABCD systems are very well-reproduced by all the test cases, with a relative error of less than 5%, which is as good as the single fits. The AB systems show a good reproduction in the density but appear as an extreme case of ABCD composition, with two monomer type proportions set to zero. As the goal of a combined potential is to be transferable to various compositions, the AB results remain acceptable in this limit. A particular case is CTV, which is the only ABC polymer that shows a relative error greater than 5% and does not contain the large styrene monomer. The inclusion of a large monomer in the CG combination may then not be used for simulating systems that do not contain it. The differences between the six test cases are small, which is a very good result from the combination of CG potentials. This shows that the STM combination is not very sensitive to the number of reference systems. As a consequence, only a few reference systems with equal proportions of the different monomers can be used to generate efficient combined CG potentials. As our goal is to minimize the number of reference MD trajectories, we show that only a small number of system compositions (test cases M1 and M2) can be used to accurately reproduce a large range of copolymer compositions. Therefore, we conclude that every composition of a ABCD copolymer (and even ABC) can be optimally reproduced at the mesoscale by using the STM combination method.

4. CONCLUSIONS We have proposed an extension of the STM coarse graining method to the case of statistical copolymers containing up to four different monomers. We have applied this method on different chemical compositions of the SBR type polymer using a combination of various CG potentials generated from reference chain compositions. Several test cases have been selected to extract the best methodology both in terms of the simulation time (small number of reference simulations) and accuracy.
